agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Order cancellation is a critical lifecycle operation in logistics. It intersects fulfillment state, payment processing, courier dispatch, and inventory management. The spec 'users should be able to cancel their order' is dangerously vague — cancellation behavior is entirely dependent on how far the order has progressed through the fulfillment pipeline. A cancellation before pick-up is operationally trivial; a cancellation mid-transit is a reverse-logistics event with real cost.",
    "bounded_context": "Order Management / Order Lifecycle",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Order", "definition": "A customer's request for goods/services to be picked up and delivered to a destination." },
      { "term": "Order Status", "definition": "The current lifecycle state of an order (e.g., Pending, Confirmed, Assigned, Picked Up, In Transit, Delivered, Cancelled)." },
      { "term": "Cancellation", "definition": "A customer-initiated request to terminate an order before its terminal state (Delivered). Not the same as a return or a refusal." },
      { "term": "Cancellation Window", "definition": "The set of order statuses during which cancellation is permitted. Business policy, not a physical constraint." },
      { "term": "Cancellation Fee", "definition": "A charge applied when cancellation occurs after the platform has incurred irrecoverable cost (e.g., courier dispatched)." },
      { "term": "Refund", "definition": "The return of payment to the customer upon successful cancellation, potentially reduced by a cancellation fee." },
      { "term": "Courier Assignment", "definition": "The binding of a courier to an order for fulfillment. Cancellation after assignment has operational cost." },
      { "term": "Terminal State", "definition": "An order state from which no further transitions are possible: Delivered, Cancelled." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Only the customer who placed the order may initiate a customer-side cancellation.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Allowing arbitrary users to cancel others' orders is a data integrity and security violation. This is an ownership constraint, not a policy.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given user A placed order 123 and user B attempts to cancel order 123, the system must reject the request with an authorization error."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "An order in a terminal state (Delivered, Cancelled) cannot be cancelled.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Terminal states are by definition final. Cancelling a delivered order is a 'return', not a 'cancellation' — different bounded context. Cancelling an already-cancelled order is idempotent at best, corrupt at worst.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an order with status Delivered or Cancelled, when the customer requests cancellation, the system must reject the request and return an error indicating the order is in a terminal state."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "An order that has been picked up by a courier (status: Picked Up or In Transit) requires special handling for cancellation — the courier must be notified and the package return flow initiated.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A physical package is in a courier's possession. Cancelling the digital record without addressing the physical reality creates a split-brain between system state and the real world. This is a physical constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an order with status Picked Up or In Transit, when cancelled, the system must create a return-to-sender task and notify the assigned courier."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Cancellation must record the timestamp, the actor (customer), and the order status at the time of cancellation for audit purposes.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Audit trail is a data integrity requirement. Without it, disputes cannot be resolved and financial reconciliation breaks.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given any successful cancellation, the cancellation record must contain: order_id, cancelled_by (user_id), cancelled_at (UTC timestamp), and status_at_cancellation."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "Cancellation fee may apply based on the order status at the time of cancellation. The fee schedule is a business policy.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Fee amounts and thresholds are business policy decisions that can change. A VP could waive all cancellation fees tomorrow.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an order with status Assigned (courier dispatched), when cancelled, the system applies the configured cancellation fee for that status tier."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A refund must be issued for the order amount minus any applicable cancellation fee upon successful cancellation.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Refund policy is a business decision. Full refunds, partial refunds, or credit-only refunds are all valid policy choices.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given a successful cancellation, the refund amount equals (order_total - cancellation_fee) and a refund record is created."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "When an order with an assigned courier is cancelled, the courier's assignment must be released so they are available for new orders.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Failing to release the courier creates a resource leak — the courier appears busy for a non-existent order. This is system state integrity.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given an order with status Assigned is cancelled, the courier's availability status must be updated to available within the same transaction."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [
    {
      "id": "AC-1",
      "given": "A customer has an order in status Pending or Confirmed (pre-dispatch)",
      "when": "The customer requests cancellation",
      "then": "The order status transitions to Cancelled, a full refund is initiated, and a cancellation confirmation is sent to the customer."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-2",
      "given": "A customer has an order in status Assigned (courier dispatched but not yet picked up)",
      "when": "The customer requests cancellation",
      "then": "The order status transitions to Cancelled, the courier assignment is released, the courier is notified, the applicable cancellation fee is applied, and a refund of (order_total - fee) is initiated."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-3",
      "given": "A customer has an order in status Picked Up or In Transit",
      "when": "The customer requests cancellation",
      "then": "The system either (a) rejects the cancellation and informs the customer to use the returns process, OR (b) initiates a return-to-sender flow with appropriate fees. [AMBIGUOUS — see escalation E-1]"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-4",
      "given": "A customer has an order in status Delivered or Cancelled",
      "when": "The customer requests cancellation",
      "then": "The system rejects the request with a clear error message: 'Order cannot be cancelled because it is already [Delivered/Cancelled].'"
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-5",
      "given": "A user who did not place the order",
      "when": "They attempt to cancel that order",
      "then": "The system returns a 403 Forbidden error. No state change occurs. The attempt is logged."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-6",
      "given": "A customer cancels an order with an assigned courier",
      "when": "The cancellation is processed",
      "then": "The courier receives a real-time notification that the assignment has been cancelled, and the courier is released back to the available pool."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-7",
      "given": "A customer requests cancellation of a valid, cancellable order",
      "when": "The cancellation succeeds",
      "then": "An audit record is persisted containing: order_id, cancelled_by, cancelled_at (UTC), status_at_cancellation, cancellation_fee_applied, and refund_amount."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-8",
      "given": "Two concurrent cancellation requests for the same order",
      "when": "Both are processed",
      "then": "Exactly one succeeds and returns success. The other receives an error indicating the order is already cancelled. No double-refund occurs."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-9",
      "given": "A customer requests cancellation but the payment provider's refund call fails",
      "when": "The refund fails",
      "then": "The order is still marked Cancelled (cancellation and refund are not atomically coupled), but a failed-refund event is emitted for reconciliation, and the customer is informed that the refund is being processed."
    },
    {
      "id": "AC-10",
      "given": "A customer cancels an order that is transitioning status (e.g., courier just picked up the package)",
      "when": "The cancellation request arrives during a status transition race",
      "then": "The system uses the order's committed status at the time of processing (not the requested status). If the order has moved past the cancellation window, the cancellation is rejected."
    }
  ],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Concurrent cancellation: two requests arrive simultaneously for the same order", "expected_behavior": "Exactly one cancellation succeeds via optimistic locking or equivalent. No double refund. Second request receives 'already cancelled' error." },
    { "scenario": "Race condition: customer cancels while courier marks order as picked up at the same moment", "expected_behavior": "The system must resolve based on committed state. If pick-up commits first, cancellation is rejected (or triggers return flow). If cancellation commits first, courier's pick-up confirmation fails with 'order cancelled' error." },
    { "scenario": "Refund provider is temporarily unavailable", "expected_behavior": "Cancellation proceeds (order moves to Cancelled), refund is queued for retry. Customer is informed refund is pending. A reconciliation job ensures eventual consistency." },
    { "scenario": "Customer cancels, then immediately places an identical new order", "expected_behavior": "These are independent orders. The cancellation of the first must not affect the second. No correlation or deduplication should be applied across distinct order IDs." },
    { "scenario": "Order has a promotional discount or coupon applied", "expected_behavior": "Refund is based on the amount actually charged, not the pre-discount price. Coupon/promo code reinstatement policy must be defined (is the coupon returned to the customer's account?)." },
    { "scenario": "Partial order cancellation (multi-item order, cancel one item)", "expected_behavior": "Spec does not address partial cancellation. If only full-order cancellation is supported, the system must clearly communicate this. If partial is needed, it's a separate feature. [See escalation E-2]" },
    { "scenario": "Cancellation requested via API by an automated integration (not a human user in a UI)", "expected_behavior": "Same business rules apply. API consumers must authenticate and be authorized as the order owner. Rate limiting should prevent abuse." },
    { "scenario": "Order is in a payment-pending state (authorization hold, not yet captured)", "expected_behavior": "Cancellation should release the authorization hold rather than issuing a refund. Different payment operation, same user-facing outcome." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Cancellation directly impacts revenue (lost sale), refund processing costs, and potential cancellation fee revenue. Poor cancellation UX drives customers to chargebacks, which cost 2-3x more than a managed refund. A well-designed cancellation flow reduces chargeback rates and builds trust.",
    "operational": "Cancellations after courier assignment waste courier time and fuel. High cancellation rates at the Assigned/Picked Up stage indicate a UX or timing problem. Cancellation metrics should feed back into dispatch optimization (e.g., delay dispatch until confirmation window closes).",
    "user": "Cancellation is a trust signal. Making it easy and transparent increases customer confidence to place orders. Hiding or complicating cancellation drives users to competitors. Clear status communication reduces cancellation requests born from uncertainty.",
    "risk": "Primary risks: (1) double-refund from race conditions, (2) orphaned courier assignments after cancellation, (3) refund fraud via repeated cancel-and-reorder patterns, (4) regulatory risk if cancellation rights under consumer protection laws are not met (e.g., EU Consumer Rights Directive mandates 14-day withdrawal).",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Gain ability to cancel orders. Trust increases if process is transparent. Frustration if cancellation window is unclear or fees are surprising." },
      { "group": "Couriers", "impact": "Cancellations after assignment waste their time. They need real-time notification and fair compensation policy for cancelled assignments." },
      { "group": "Finance/Accounting", "impact": "Must reconcile refunds, cancellation fees, and partial payments. Need audit trail for dispute resolution." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Cancellation is a top-3 support ticket category. Self-service cancellation reduces support volume. Edge cases (post-pickup, refund failures) will still generate tickets." },
      { "group": "Product/Growth", "impact": "Cancellation rate is a key health metric. High rates signal product or pricing issues. Cancellation reasons data is valuable for product improvement." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 40,
    "reasoning": "The spec is a single sentence with no qualification of scope, lifecycle boundaries, fee policy, or post-pickup behavior. Multiple critical business decisions are left entirely unspecified. I can define the domain structure and invariants with high confidence, but the actual business policy choices (cancellation window, fee schedule, partial cancellation support, post-pickup behavior) are pure ambiguity. Shipping acceptance criteria with guessed policies would be worse than escalating.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Order lifecycle states and terminal state constraints (BR-2) — these are universal in logistics",
      "Authorization: only the order owner can cancel (BR-1)",
      "Audit trail requirement (BR-4)",
      "Courier release on cancellation (BR-7)",
      "Concurrency edge cases — these are engineering invariants"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Cancellation window — which statuses allow cancellation? Especially Picked Up and In Transit",
      "Fee schedule — what fees apply at each stage? Are there any?",
      "Refund mechanism — full refund, partial, store credit?",
      "Partial cancellation — is multi-item partial cancel in scope?",
      "Post-pickup cancellation — reject outright or trigger return flow?",
      "Coupon/promo reinstatement policy on cancellation",
      "Regulatory requirements (jurisdiction-dependent cancellation rights)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The order lifecycle includes at minimum: Pending, Confirmed, Assigned, Picked Up, In Transit, Delivered, Cancelled",
      "Payment has been captured or authorized at order creation time",
      "Couriers are notifiable in real-time",
      "This is full-order cancellation, not partial (assumption due to spec silence)",
      "Cancellation is customer-initiated only (admin/support cancellation is a separate flow)"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not define the cancellation window. Can a customer cancel after the courier has picked up the package? After the package is in transit? The business must decide: is post-pickup cancellation (a) rejected outright, (b) allowed with a fee, or (c) converted to a return-to-sender flow? Each option has radically different operational and financial implications.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Couriers", "Finance/Accounting", "Operations"],
      "options": [
        "A: Allow cancellation only before courier pickup (Pending, Confirmed, Assigned statuses)",
        "B: Allow cancellation at any pre-delivery status, with escalating fees",
        "C: Allow cancellation at any pre-delivery status, but post-pickup triggers a return-to-sender flow instead of instant cancellation"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A for MVP — it is operationally simple and avoids the reverse-logistics complexity. Post-pickup cancellation can be a Phase 2 feature. Recommend: A."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The spec does not address partial cancellation. If an order contains multiple items, can the customer cancel individual items or only the entire order? Partial cancellation requires partial refund calculation, potential re-routing, and updated courier instructions.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Product/Growth", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A: Full-order cancellation only (MVP)",
        "B: Support item-level cancellation within an order"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A for MVP. Partial cancellation is a significant feature with its own domain complexity. Recommend: A."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No cancellation fee schedule has been defined. The spec does not indicate whether cancellation is free, whether fees apply at certain stages, or what the fee amounts are. This is a business policy decision that cannot be inferred from engineering context.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Accounting", "Product/Growth"],
      "options": [
        "A: No cancellation fees (customer-friendly, higher cancellation rates expected)",
        "B: Tiered fees based on fulfillment stage (e.g., free before dispatch, $X after dispatch)",
        "C: Flat cancellation fee regardless of stage"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B — tiered fees align cost to the platform with the actual operational cost incurred. Free before dispatch, reasonable fee after courier assignment. Recommend: B."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No refund mechanism specified. Should refunds go back to the original payment method, to store credit, or offer the customer a choice? What is the refund SLA? This affects payment integration scope.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Accounting", "Engineering"],
      "options": [
        "A: Always refund to original payment method",
        "B: Offer choice between original method and store credit",
        "C: Refund as store credit only"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option A — refund to original payment method. This is the customer expectation and the legal default in most jurisdictions. Recommend: A."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
